title: Linguine al pesto
subtitle: Linguine with basil, pine nut, and parmesan pesto
excerpt: Linguine tossed with a pesto of fresh basil, pine nuts, garlic, parmesan, pecorino, and olive oil pounded in a mortar. The Genoese sauce in its proper form — bright green, slightly coarse in texture, and made just before the pasta is ready.
sourceNotes: Based on the Ligurian pesto Genovese tradition as documented in Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992) and Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006). Traditional Genoese pesto is made with small-leaved Ligurian basil, which is milder than large-leaved varieties; this recipe uses standard supermarket basil.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pesto keeps refrigerated under a thin film of olive oil for 3 days.

content.text: Pesto Genovese made in a food processor is faster and perfectly acceptable; pesto made in a mortar is different in character. The mortar bruises rather than cuts the basil, releasing different aromatic compounds and producing a sauce with a more complex, slightly grassy flavour. Both methods work; the mortar version is worth the ten minutes of effort when the basil is good.

The pasta water is the critical element at the end. Pesto should never be heated directly, as it turns dark and loses its flavour, but a ladleful of starchy pasta water loosens it and creates an emulsified coating that clings to the linguine. Toss off the heat, quickly, and serve immediately.

Pound {{garlic}} garlic clove with a pinch of {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a mortar until a paste forms. Add {{pine-nuts}} of pine nuts and pound until roughly broken. Add {{basil}} basil leaves in small batches, pounding and rotating after each addition until a rough paste forms. Mix in {{parmesan}} grated parmesan and {{pecorino}} pecorino. Stir in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Alternatively, combine everything in a food processor and pulse to a coarse paste.

Cook {{linguine}} of linguine in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Scoop out a large mug of pasta water before draining. In a large bowl, mix the pesto with 3–4 tablespoons of hot pasta water to loosen. Drain the pasta and tip into the bowl. Toss quickly and thoroughly to coat. Add more pasta water if needed — the sauce should cling without clumping. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Heat applied to the pesto, or basil bruised too aggressively
items.fix: Never heat pesto directly. Mix it with pasta water off the heat. The pasta's residual warmth is enough.

items.symptom: Pesto is too thick to coat the pasta
items.fix: Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time, tossing after each addition, until the pesto coats the pasta evenly.
Use young, small-leaved basil where possible. Avoid bruising the basil until you are ready to use it.

content.text: Pesto Genovese is the defining sauce of Liguria, the narrow coastal strip between the Alps and the Mediterranean that wraps around Genoa. The basil grown there, small-leaved and sheltered from direct sun by the terrain, has a sweetness and mildness that the larger varieties found elsewhere cannot quite match. In Genoa, pesto appears over linguine and trenette with green beans and potato; it goes over gnocchi; it flavours the local minestrone. Outside Liguria, the pasta preparation is the most familiar form.
